text: This verse is more easily understood if we suppose (as is most likely) that the Israelites were not clear in their own minds whether the hill-shrines, etc. were devoted to the worship of the true God or not.

Literally, ‘For now they will be saying, We have no king, because we do not fear God; what will the king do to (or, for) us? For now they speak perjured words in making a treaty; therefore judgement springs up in their furrows like hemlock’. It is hard to feel certain about the meaning or bearing of the passage.
‘Calf’; so the Septuagint Greek. The Hebrew text, like the Latin version, gives ‘heifers’, followed by a masculine singular pronoun in the rest of the sentence.
Cf. 9.9 above. Here, as there, some think the reference is to Jg. 20; but it may easily be to some incident not elsewhere recorded.
The last part of this verse is usually translated, ‘Juda shall plough, and Jacob shall break the clods for himself’. But the expression ‘for himself’ seems curiously inappropriate.
The verb used in the Hebrew text may mean either ‘rain down’ or ‘teach’.
‘Shameful furrows’; it can hardly be a coincidence that the Hebrew verb used can mean either ‘ploughing’ or ‘carving images’.
According to the Hebrew text ‘as Salman (quite differently spelt) destroyed Beth-arbel’, an event not elsewhere alluded to.
